Hearing is held. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows: (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

PLAINTIFFS SEIU-USWW, AN UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATION, JUAN MARTINEZ'S Motion And Motion For Attorney's Fees

Plaintiffs' motion for attorney fees on appeal is granted in the amount of $198,884 ($142,060 x 1.4 multiplier).
Defendants argue that the $142,060 lodestar is unreasonable and that a multiplier is unwarranted. I disagree on both counts.
Based on my 36 years of experience with appellate work in the San Francisco Bay Area, $142,060 for taking a case of this magnitude through appeal is reasonable. (Serrano v. Priest (1977) 20 Cal.3d 25, 49.) Defendants' only specific complaint is "duplicated efforts," because more than one plaintiff attorney strategized, analyzed and reviewed. (Opp. 4:11-16.) However, the practice of law is a collaborative effort - as was no doubt reflected in defense counsel's work for their own client.

A 1.4 multiplier is warranted for at least two reasons. First, the appeal benefited the public interest by producing a published decision on the Displaced Janitor Opportunity and Displaced Worker Protection acts. (Coalition for L.A. County Planning in the Public Interest v. Board of Supervisors (1977) 76 Cal.App.3d 241, 251.) Second, counsel obtained an excellent result for their clients on novel issues. (City of Oakland v. Oakland Raiders (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 78, 82-83.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com mailto:maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Class Certification
Off Calendar May-31-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Atty. Alexander Nazarav & Atty. Jannah V. Manansala of Weinberg Roger & Rosenfeld, Tel. No. 1-510-337-1001 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. Robert Goodin & Atty. keith Johnson of Goodin Macbride Squeri & Day LLP, Tel. No. 1-415-392-7900 appearing for the Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Plaintiff Juan Martinez's motion for class certification is granted. The following class is certified: thirty janitors and handymen, who had been employed by VPM Maintenance Management, LLC for more than eight months as of April 10, 2015 when their employment with VPM ended and who were not subsequently retained by defendant Preferred Buildings Services, Inc. Mr. Martinez's request to act as a class representative is granted. Mr. Martinez has shown that the class is ascertainable. The class is sufficiently numerous. A common issue of law - whether Preferred violated the law by not retaining Mr. Martinez and the other members of the class - predominates and is based on a set of facts that is common to all class members. The possibility that one of class members may not be eligible to receive damages or that the determination of the amount of damages will be based on individual assessments does not show that individual issues predominate or otherwise preclude certification. (Sav-on Drug Stores, Inc. v. Superior Court (2004) 34 Cal.4th 319, 333 ("a class action is not inappropriate simply because each member of the class may at some point be required to make an individual showing as to his or her eligibility for recovery or as to the amount of his or her damages.")). There is no evidence that Preferred's waiver defense requires individualized determinations of fact as to each class member's intent when signing a severance agreement because the Union that negotiated all the agreements, explained the agreements to the workers, the agreements' terms were identical and the contracting party to all of the agreements states that it did not bargain for a waiver of statutory rights. Mr. Martinez has shown that his claim is typical of the class and he can adequately represent the class. While Preferred's theory of the case is that Mr. Martinez is not entitled to recover because any damages resulting from Preferred's conduct has been offset by the amount of Mr. Martinez's severance package, there is no showing that this theory is "a defense unique to the class representative [and] will be a major focus of the litigation" or that it renders Mr. Martinez's interests "antagonistic to or in conflict with the objectives of those ' he purports to represent." (Medrazo v. Honda of North Hollywood (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 89, 99). Class treatment serves judicial economy by avoiding litigation of similar issues as well as benefits the class by enabling workers, less able to litigate individually due to linguistic barriers and limited comprehension of the legal issues, to obtain recovery. The court encouraged the parties to file for a single assignment or complex litigation. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR #8600

Off Calendar Aug-30-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:

Charles Birenbaum, Esq. (415-655-1310) of GreenbergTraurig for Defendant PREFERRED BUILDING SERVICES INC.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces the basis for the tentative rulings. Plaintiff argues. Defendant argues intent, preemption. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling with additional language as follows:

DEFENDANT PREFERRED BUILDING SERVICES INC.'s SECOND MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or Alternatively, Motion For Summary Adjudication

Defendant Preferred Building Services, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment, or alternatively, motion for summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. Preferred has not satisfied its summary judgment/adjudication burden of presenting facts showing that either or both of the claims alleged by plaintiffs SEIU-USWW and Juan Martinez are preempted by section 301 of the Labor management and Relations Act. The two claims are not "substantially dependent" on the Side letter and the Side Letter does not need to be "interpreted" to adjudicate those claims. There are triable disputes whether: a) plaintiffs waived their claims; b) Preferred is excused from complying with Labor Code 1060 et seq. and San Francisco Police Code 3300C.1 et seq; c) the affected employees are entitled to benefits under those statutory schemes; and d) SEIU acted with unclean hands.

In addition, The Court affirms the affirmative defense as to Impossibility and supersedes the prior orders on the waiver and release. There are triable issues of fact to be determined at the trial.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312 and in compliance with CCP 437c(g).

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Jun-02-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. CAITLIN GRAY & JANNAH MANANSALA OF WEINBERG ROGER & ROSENFELD, TEL. NO. 1-510-337-1001 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. ROBERT A. GOODIN OF GOODIN, MACBRIDE & LAMPHREY LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-392-7900 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.
RULING - A R G U E D; The court modified its tentative ruling and ruled as follows: PLAINTIFFS SEIU-USWW and JUAN MARTINEZ'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION is DENIED as to the first cause of action for violation of the Displaced Janitor Opportunity Act ("DJOA") and GRANTED as to the third, fourth and twelfth affirmative defenses of waiver, release and impossible compliance with statute. Defendant Preferred Building Services, Inc.'s preemption argument based on section 301 of the LMRA lacks merit. SEIU's claim for violation of the DJOA is independent of the Side Letter. The Side Letter does not need to be "interpreted" to adjudicate the DJOA claim. The Side Letter states that DJOA rights are not waived and the court merely needs to apply that statement. SEIU fails to satisfy its burden of production on its DJOA claim. Exhibit C to the Gray declaration is a roster that purportedly lists the janitors' pay rates. Preferred's objection to that exhibit as hearsay and lack of foundation is sustained. SEIU fails to show that the business records exception applies. Exhibits DD (pay records), EE (pay records), and FF (spread sheet) suffer from the same infirmities and thus Preferred's objections to those exhibits are also sustained. In addition, Preferred's response to undisputed material fact 9 creates a triable issue of material fact regarding the scope of SEIU's claim and the amount of damages. Preferred's third and fourth affirmative defenses of waiver and release fail as a matter of law. The only reasonable reading of the Side Letter is that the employees did not waive or release their rights under the DJOA. The employees' "resignations" did not displace their rights under the DJOA, which is liberally construed. Preferred's twelfth affirmative defense of impossibility also fails. Preferred admits that it never requested a list of the former employees from VPM or Parkmerced. (UMF 27.) SEIU further shows that such a list was available. (UMF 28 (Bricker Depo., 45:5-15).) Preferred does not meaningfully dispute UMF 28. Preferred's objection that the existence of the list is speculative and lacks foundation is overruled. SEIU's untimely reply evidence is not considered. (San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308, 316.) Order signed in open court. Off Calendar Mar-08-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Attys. JANNAH MANASALA, ESQ. and ALEGANDRO DELGADO, ESQ. of Weinberg, Roger & Rosenfeld APC, Tel No. 510-337-1001 appearing for the Plaintiffs;
Attys. ROBERT GOODIN, ESQ. and KEITH E. JOHNSON, ESQ. of Goodin, MacBridge, Squeri & Day, LLP, Tel No. 415-392-7900 appearing for Defendant Preferred Building Services Inc.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. As to DEFENDANT PREFERRED BUILDING SERVICES INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication, Defendant Preferred Building Services' motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant did not sustain its initial burden of production. Defendant's facts 3, 4 and 9 state that plaintiffs' last day of work at Parkmerced was the same day that VPPM's contract ended and defendant's contract began four days later. Under these facts, plaintiffs retain their status as "employees" per Labor Code 1061 and San Francisco Police Code 3300C.2 even though they entered into separation agreements with VPM providing that they resigned their employment in exchange for severance benefits. Based on the facts presented by defendant, nothing in the severance agreements constituted a waiver by plaintiffs of their rights under Labor Code 1061 and San Francisco Police Code 3300C.2 or was intended by the parties to those agreements to preclude plaintiffs from asserting their rights under those provisions.
